system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 3: Jazz Standard Study #2: Blue Bossa
Pages 40-51 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

The next standard in our studies is the classic Kenny Dorham tune
Blue Bossa. It’s a smooth transition from Autumn Leaves because it
also maintains a fairly basic harmonic structure, although this time it
has a key center twist which we will talk about.

Blue Besse Bio

Blue Bossa was written by jazz trumpeter Kenny Dorham. It was first recorded
on Joe Henderson’s “Page One” album in 1963. It’s a blend of hard bop and
Bossa Noua. It is most commonly played in the key of concert C minor.

While Blue Bossa isn’t a classic Bossa Nova tune like the many com-
positions of Antonio Carlos Jobim, it demonstrates how important the
relationship between jazz and Bossa Nova is. As a jazz musician you
need to have some Bossa Nova’s under your belt, and Blue Bossa is a
great entry-level tune to get your feet wet.

It’s important to note that when it comes to jazz standards, many
of them are played with a swing feel, meaning the eighth notes are
swung. However, in the case of a Bossa Nova, the eighth notes are
played straight.

Blue Bossa is a great entry-level tune because the melody is relatively
simple, the form is short, and the chord changes are minimal. We can
learn a lot from this song.


>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Joe Henderson from “Page One” 1963

Dexter Gordon from “Biting the Apple” 1986

Pat Martino from “Cream” 2009

Milt Jackson, Joe Pass, Ray Brown from “The Big 3”
1976

Let’s jump into understanding this song better. The next several pages
will include the chord map, the guide tone chart, and the chords anal-
ysis. Be sure to work through each individually, especially the guide
tones, and then I'll talk further about the harmony and how it works.

Let’s talk about the harmonic structure of Blue Bossa. Let’s define
the color coding first:

‘Red = the parent minor key.
Orange = aii-V to the relative major (an interpretation).
Purple =a shift to the key of Db major.

The first thing to focus on is the key center, which is C minor. Even
though in bars 10-13 there is a key change to Db major, the parent key
revolves around C minor, and it pretty much sticks to it.

The next thing to pay attention to is that Blue Bossa is a 16 bar form.
This isn’t entirely uncommon, but not as common as a 12 bar, 24, or
32 bar form as you will see in many of the other jazz standards we will
study. It’s a small detail, but something worth noting.

Similar to Autumn Leaves we are mostly dealing with major and minor
ii-V or ii-V-I chord progressions. However, let’s look at the first area of
question in bars 3-4.

Fain’ BY”

iv VII

or ii-V of Relative Major - ----------

Preceding the Fmin7 is a Cmin7. It’s very common to move from the |
to the IV chord in a major or minor key. For example, this is something
you see all of the time in a major or minor blues. But the Bb7 that fol-
lows it is why | have highlighted this area as one to pay attention to.

There are two ways to look at this. | suggest you think about both ap-
proaches. The most accurate description of the relationship of these
two chords to the rest of the progression is the iv-VII relationship to
the parent key center. But you can also consider those two chords as
a ii-V of the relative major.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 46 |



What's the relative major in this case? Eb major. Now you see why un-
derstanding relative keys were important in our last study, right?

Regardless of whether you look at these two chords as a iv-VI or a ii-V
to the relative major, they sound good because they have intentional
harmonic movement and they are entirely diatonic.

The next section in question is the big key change. Let’s take a look.

EP? a Duss

#  ji-V-lin Db Major ------------------------------------------------
(New Key Center)

To be clear, this is a key center change because Db major is by no
means diatonic to C minor. What makes this even more apparent is
that it’s a ii-V-I, which is textbook diatonic moving harmony.

The question is: why a half step above the parent key center? This
could only be answered by Kenny Dorham himself, but if you listen to
the melody, it fits perfectly. It doesn’t sound forced or out of place.
It’s clear that when Dorham composed this song, he used the melody
to determining the direction.

This half step movement in key centers is also not abrasive. It doesn’t
take long for the ear to adjust to a half step movement, and as long as
the melody flows logically, it’s going to sound good.

The truth is, any chord can follow any chord as long as there is a
strong melody in place. This goes for improvisation as well. You could
even play outside of the chord changes, but if your improvised melody
is strong and you resolve it confidently, it’s going to sound intentional
and pleasing to the ear.

This leads us perfectly into our next improv lesson!

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



IMPROV LESSON
Using the Melody in Your Felos

In our last improv lesson on Autumn Leaves we talked about the guide
tones, and how knowing what the important notes in the chords are,
combined with voice leading, will help you outline the changes.

This is definitely part of the puzzle when it comes to becoming a great
jazz improviser. But at the end of the day, we are trying to make melo-
dies. A melodic solo, regardless of chops or technique, is the ultimate
goal.

So how do we start developing melody in our improvisation? Well, we
will be covering quite a bit of this as we move through other improv les-
sons in this book, but hands down the place to start is the melody of
the song you are playing.

Often times, improvisers forget that they are improvising over a song
and not just a set of chord changes. The melody of a song is the de-
fining part. | mean, people aren’t usually singing chord changes in the
shower, right? Melodies are copyrightable, but chord changes are not.
What makes one blues different from another blues? The melody. The
chord changes are usually the same or similar.

| often get questions from my subscribers on Learn Jazz Standards.
com asking what scales they can play over different chords or chord
progressions. While that’s not a bad question to ask, the intent is usu-
ally to figure out what notes they can play over a piece of music. But
why not just look at the melody first? | think this is the best and most
musical starting point.

I’m going to teach you a great exercise to help you put this into action.
I’m also going to have a notated example of this exercise so that you
can further understand what | am getting at.

Blue Bossa is a great tune to apply this improv lesson because it has
a solid, simple melody. In this exercise, you will be improvising over Blue
Bossa using the melody as your guide. Feel free to use the backing
track provided in your download folder, a metronome, or practice with
another musician.


HERE’S THE EXERCISE:

1. The first chorus, just play the melody completely straight.
Essentially, there is no improvising happening yet. During this
chorus, you are establishing exactly what the melody is.

2. The second chorus, embellish the melody. What does it mean
to embellish? You are using the melody as a “guideline.” You can
veer off from the melody a bit, but not too far off. You want to
be clearly hearing the melody throughout the chorus, while still
taking liberties with it.

3. The third chorus, reference the melody. You can go ahead and
improvise freely and don’t necessarily have to stick so tightly
to the melody. But you will need to reference it from time to
time. Someone who just walked into your practice room should
be able to eventually pick up what song you’re playing because
you referenced a part of the melody.

To demonstrate, | have transcribed an improvised solo | took over Blue
Bossa using this exercise. The first chorus | just play the melody, the
second chorus | embellish (though maybe a little too much), and the
third chorus | reference. You can follow along with the music by clicking
the sheet music to listen to the recording.

Keep in mind, | improvised this solo on a guitar so there may be areas
where it violates the range of your instrument. However, | have marked
optional suggestions of playing a line an octave lower. This is merely a
demonstration so that you can understand how to do this for yourself.
